channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Construction and characterization of measures in block coherence resource theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research advances our fundamental understanding of quantum coherence by developing more sophisticated mathematical tools to measure and compare this essential quantum resource. The work introduces new families of coherence measures based on well-established information-theoretic concepts, providing researchers with a broader toolkit for quantifying coherence in complex quantum systems where traditional measures may fall short. Particularly intriguing is the application to the Kominis master equation, which governs certain chemical reactions, demonstrating how these theoretical advances could help us better understand and harness quantum effects in biological and chemical processes. While highly mathematical, this foundational work could ultimately enable more precise optimization of quantum algorithms and better characterization of quantum advantage in various applications.

Quantum coherence, as a direct manifestation of the quantum superposition principle, is a crucial resource in quantum information processing. Block coherence resource theory generalizes the traditional coherence framework by defining coherence via a set of orthogonal projectors. Within this framework, we investigates the construction and comparison of block coherence measures. First, we propose two universal methods for constructing coherence measures and introduce a two-parameter family of measures based on the $α$-$z$ Rényi relative entropy and a family of measures based on the Tsallis relative operator entropy. Second, through theoretical proofs and numerical counterexamples, we compares the ordering relations and numerical magnitudes among different block coherence measures and establishes a series of universal numerical inequalities to constrain their values. Besides, we also use $C_{α,1}$ to show the role of coherence in complex dynamic evolution of the Kominis master equation that includes recombination reactions.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qubit-efficient embedding of parity-encoded Hamiltonians in quantum annealers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical bottleneck in quantum annealing by developing an efficient way to map parity-encoded problems onto D-Wave's latest Zephyr architecture. The Sourlas-Lechner-Hauke-Zoller encoding scheme offers theoretical advantages for quantum annealing but has been held back by the challenge of efficiently embedding these problems onto real hardware graphs. By reducing the qubit overhead to just three qubits per logical spin (compared to higher requirements on older Pegasus chips) and providing systematic embedding rules, this work brings us closer to practically implementing this promising encoding scheme on current quantum annealers. This could unlock new problem-solving capabilities and improve the efficiency of quantum annealing for certain optimization problems.

The Sourlas-Lechner-Hauke-Zoller (SLHZ) scheme for quantum annealing uses the parity to encode logical variables and has several advantages, but it has not been implemented for large-scale quantum annealers. If the SLHZ-based approach can be implemented on currently available quantum annealers, we can evaluate its performance. An efficient method to embed the parity-encoded model into the hardware graphs of available quantum annealers is one of the key elements for this approach. We propose a qubit-efficient embedding scheme for parity-encoded Hamiltonians on quantum annealers with the Zephyr connectivity. We give an explicit constructive embedding of the interaction graph of an intermediate Hamiltonian, which contains only one- and two-body interactions, into the Zephyr graph. Our embedding maps each spin to a two-qubit chain using systematic chain-assignment rules. Its validity is verified via the resulting chain-to-chain connectivity. Our embedding also offers practical flexibility. Chains assigned to ancillary spins allow reduction to a single physical qubit, leading to options to avoid inactive qubits. The number of required qubits per spin in the parity Hamiltonian is three, which is fewer than that for a known embedding scheme for the Pegasus graph.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Hunting for quantum advantage in electronic structure calculations is a highly non-trivial task
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work delivers a reality check for quantum computing's promised advantage in chemistry simulations by demonstrating that classical algorithms can tackle previously "quantum-only" problems with remarkable efficiency. The researchers successfully computed electronic structures for complex iron-sulfur clusters that were recently added to IBM and RIKEN's Quantum Advantage Tracker, using advanced density matrix renormalization group methods to handle systems with nearly 90 electrons across over 100 orbitals. This benchmark establishes a new high bar for classical performance in strongly correlated quantum chemistry problems, forcing the quantum computing community to look beyond current target molecules when searching for genuine quantum advantage. The results underscore a crucial point often overlooked in the quantum hype: as classical algorithms continue advancing rapidly, the moving goalposts for quantum supremacy make identifying practical quantum advantage increasingly challenging.

In light of major developments over the past decades in both quantum computing and simulations on classical hardware, it is a serious challenge to identify a real-world problem where quantum advantage is expected to appear. In quantum chemistry, electronic structure calculations of strongly correlated, i.e. multi-reference problems, are often argued to fall into such category because of their intractability with standard methods based on mean-field theory. Therefore, providing state-of-the-art benchmark data by classical algorithms is necessary to make a decisive conclusion when such competing development directions are compared. We report cutting-edge performance results together with high accuracy ground state energy for the Fe$_4$S$_4$ molecular cluster on a CAS(54,36) model space, a problem that has been included quite recently among the list of systems in the {\it Quantum Advantage Tracker} webpage maintained by IBM and RIKEN. Pushing the limits even further, we also present CAS-SCF based orbital optimizations for unprecedented CAS sizes of up to 89 electrons in 102 orbitals [CAS(89,102)] for the Fe$_5$S$_{12}$H$_4^{5-}$ molecular system comprising twenty five open shell orbitals in its sextet ground state and an active spaces size of 331 electrons in 451 orbitals. We have achieved our results via mixed-precision spin-adapted \textit{ab initio} Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) electronic structure calculations interfaced with the ORCA program package and utilizing the NVIDIA Blackwell graphics processing unit (GPU) platform. We argue that DMRG benchmark data should be taken as a classical reference when quantum advantage is reported. In addition, full exploitation of classical hardware should also be considered since even the most advanced DMRG implementations are still in a premature stage regarding utilization of all the benefits of GPU technology.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks a crucial milestone for silicon-based quantum computing, demonstrating that the same material powering conventional electronics can successfully perform logical quantum operations with error correction. The achievement is particularly significant because silicon spin qubits offer exceptional coherence times while leveraging decades of semiconductor manufacturing expertise, potentially providing a more practical path to large-scale quantum computers than other qubit technologies. By proving that silicon can handle logical operations—where multiple physical qubits work together to create more reliable logical qubits—this work validates silicon as a serious contender in the race toward fault-tolerant quantum computing. The compatibility with existing chip fabrication infrastructure could dramatically accelerate the development timeline for commercially viable quantum processors.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QCi Deploys Dirac-3 System on Quantum Corridor Network
item.description:
Curator's Take: This deployment marks a significant step toward practical quantum computing infrastructure, as QCi's Dirac-3 optimization system becomes accessible through North America's first quantum-safe commercial network. The partnership between Quantum Corridor and QCi demonstrates how quantum hardware companies are moving beyond lab demonstrations to create real commercial access points for enterprises needing quantum optimization capabilities. While QCi's photonic approach to quantum optimization differs from gate-based quantum computers, this network deployment could provide valuable real-world testing for quantum algorithms in logistics, finance, and supply chain optimization. The integration with quantum-safe communications also highlights the growing ecosystem where quantum computing and quantum cryptography technologies are being bundled together for enterprise customers.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum Corridor, the first inter-state quantum safe commercial communications network in North America, and Quantum Computing Inc. (“QCi”/Nasdaq: QUBT), an innovative, quantum optics and integrated photonics technology company, today announced the placement of a QCi Dirac-3 quantum optimization machine on the network. The partnership will allow enhanced customer access for […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Shor's algorithm is possible with as few as 10,000 reconfigurable atomic qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a major breakthrough in making quantum cryptanalysis practically feasible, dramatically reducing the hardware requirements for running Shor's algorithm from millions of qubits down to just 10,000 reconfigurable atomic qubits. The work leverages recent advances in high-rate quantum error correction and efficient circuit design to show that cryptographically relevant problems like breaking RSA-2048 encryption could be solved in days or weeks rather than requiring impossibly large quantum computers. What makes this particularly significant is that neutral atom platforms have already demonstrated many of the key components needed, including fault-tolerant operations and arrays of thousands of qubits, suggesting this timeline for practical quantum cryptanalysis may be more realistic than previously thought. This analysis will likely accelerate discussions around post-quantum cryptography migration, as the gap between current quantum hardware capabilities and cryptographically dangerous quantum computers continues to narrow.

Quantum computers have the potential to perform computational tasks beyond the reach of classical machines. A prominent example is Shor's algorithm for integer factorization and discrete logarithms, which is of both fundamental importance and practical relevance to cryptography. However, due to the high overhead of quantum error correction, optimized resource estimates for cryptographically relevant instances of Shor's algorithm require millions of physical qubits. Here, by leveraging advances in high-rate quantum error-correcting codes, efficient logical instruction sets, and circuit design, we show that Shor's algorithm can be executed at cryptographically relevant scales with as few as 10,000 reconfigurable atomic qubits. Increasing the number of physical qubits improves time efficiency by enabling greater parallelism; under plausible assumptions, the runtime for discrete logarithms on the P-256 elliptic curve could be just a few days for a system with 26,000 physical qubits, while the runtime for factoring RSA-2048 integers is one to two orders of magnitude longer. Recent neutral-atom experiments have demonstrated universal fault-tolerant operations below the error-correction threshold, computation on arrays of hundreds of qubits, and trapping arrays with more than 6,000 highly coherent qubits. Although substantial engineering challenges remain, our theoretical analysis indicates that an appropriately designed neutral-atom architecture could support quantum computation at cryptographically relevant scales. More broadly, these results highlight the capability of neutral atoms for fault-tolerant quantum computing with wide-ranging scientific and technological applications.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Tunable Nonlocal ZZ Interaction for Remote Controlled-Z Gates Between Distributed Fixed-Frequency Qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in scaling quantum computers: how to connect high-quality qubits across different physical modules without sacrificing performance. The proposed double-transmon coupler system achieves an impressive feat by enabling near-perfect entangling gates between qubits separated by 25 centimeters of cable while maintaining an exceptional on/off ratio of over a million to one, effectively eliminating unwanted crosstalk when the connection isn't needed. This modular approach could be transformative for building large-scale quantum processors, as it sidesteps the mounting engineering challenges of cramming thousands of qubits and their control lines into a single monolithic chip. The demonstrated 99.99% fidelity for remote controlled-Z gates suggests this architecture could provide a practical pathway to fault-tolerant quantum computing systems that scale beyond current limitations.

Fault-tolerant quantum computing requires large-scale superconducting processors, yet monolithic architectures face increasing constraints from wiring density, crosstalk, and fabrication yield. Modular superconducting platforms offer a scalable alternative, but achieving high-fidelity entangling gates between distant modules remains a central challenge, particularly for highly coherent fixed-frequency qubits. Here, we propose a distributed hardware architecture designed to overcome this bottleneck by employing a pair of double-transmon couplers (DTCs). By synchronously controlling the two DTCs stationed at opposite ends of a macroscopic cable, our scheme strongly suppresses residual static inter-module coupling while enabling on-demand activation of a non-local cross-Kerr interaction with an on/off ratio exceeding $10^6$. Through comprehensive system-level numerical simulations incorporating realistic hardware parameters, we demonstrate that this mechanism can realize a remote controlled-Z (CZ) gate with a fidelity over 99.99\% between fixed-frequency transmons housed in separate packages interconnected by a 25 cm coaxial cable. These results establish a highly viable, hardware-efficient route toward high-performance distributed superconducting processors.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SesQ: A Surface Electrostatic Simulator for Precise Energy Participation Ratio Simulation in Superconducting Qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical bottleneck in superconducting qubit design by introducing SesQ, a specialized simulator that dramatically improves how engineers calculate energy participation ratios - a key metric for understanding and minimizing the dielectric losses that plague quantum coherence. The breakthrough lies in SesQ's surface-based approach, which achieves roughly 100x speedup over conventional 3D finite element methods while actually delivering superior accuracy for the nanometer-scale physics that matter most in qubit performance. Perhaps most importantly, the research reveals that existing commercial simulation tools significantly underestimate energy participation ratios, suggesting that many current qubit designs may be less optimal than previously believed. This computational advance could accelerate the development of higher-quality superconducting qubits by enabling rapid design iteration and more precise optimization of the material interfaces where quantum information is most vulnerable to noise.

An accurate and efficient numerical electromagnetic model for superconducting qubits is essential for characterizing and minimizing design-dependent dielectric losses. The energy participation ratio (EPR) is the commonly adopted metric used to evaluate these losses, but its calculation presents a severe multiscale computational challenge. Conventional finite element method (FEM) requires 3D volumetric meshing, leading to prohibitive computational costs and memory requirements when attempting to capture singular electric fields at nanometer-thin material interfaces. To address this bottleneck, we propose SesQ, a surface integral equation simulator tailored for the precise simulation of the EPR. By applying discretization on 2D surfaces, deriving a semi-analytical multilayer Green's function, and employing a dedicated non-conformal boundary mesh refinement scheme, SesQ accurately resolves singular edge fields without an explosive growth in the number of unknowns. Validations with analytically solvable models demonstrate that SesQ accelerates capacitance extraction by roughly two orders of magnitude compared to commercial FEM tools. While achieving comparable accuracy for capacitance extraction, SesQ delivers superior precision for EPR calculation. Simulations of practical transmon qubits further reveal that FEM approaches tend to significantly underestimate the EPR. Finally, the high efficiency of SesQ enables rapid iteration in the layout optimization, as demonstrated by minimizing the EPR of the qubit pattern, establishing the simulator as a powerful tool for the automated design of low-loss superconducting quantum circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Probing excited-state quantum phase transitions with trapped cold ions
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a fascinating bridge between fundamental quantum many-body physics and practical trapped ion experiments, showing how a single ion in a radio-frequency trap can serve as a testbed for exploring excited-state quantum phase transitions - exotic critical phenomena that occur not at a system's ground state, but at higher energy levels. What makes this particularly compelling is the concrete experimental roadmap the researchers provide, mapping theoretical control parameters to real trapped ion setups and accounting for realistic noise effects, potentially making these elusive quantum critical phenomena accessible in near-term experiments. The work demonstrates how trapped ion platforms, already proven workhorses for quantum computing and simulation, can probe even more exotic quantum phenomena than previously realized. By identifying specific witness observables and studying how the system behaves when driven across these quantum critical points, this research opens new experimental avenues for understanding quantum criticality beyond the traditional ground-state paradigm.

We propose concrete protocols to realize quantum criticality due to excited-state quantum phase transitions (ESQPTs) experimentally in presumably the simplest and most resilient system involving a single trapped ion oscillating in a radio-frequency Paul trap. We identify a specific class of excited states of the Extended Rabi Model (ERM) Hamiltonian, which occur between two critical ESQPT energies of the model in its (anti)Jaynes-Cummings superradiant phase. Properties of these states motivate the definition of several ESQPT witness observables. We study their critical scaling behaviors as well as various distinct state evolutions by driving the system across the quantum criticalities by changing the qubit-phonon coupling strength linearly in time at different finite rates. A mapping of the theoretical control parameters of the ERM to the experimental parameters of a trapped ion setup is provided, and simulations are performed for values referencing existing state-of-the-art setups, addressing both unitary state evolutions as well as relevant open-system corrections.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Emergent-Coupling-Based Ansatz Evaluated on a Superconducting Quantum Processor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant advancement in variational quantum algorithms by introducing a physics-informed ansatz that outperforms standard hardware-efficient circuits while maintaining practical implementability on near-term quantum devices. The emergent-coupling-based ansatz cleverly uses renormalization group theory to identify the most important quantum interactions, creating shallow circuits that capture essential physics with fewer gates - a crucial advantage given current hardware limitations. The experimental demonstration on 30-qubit superconducting processors with 96.47% energy accuracy represents one of the larger-scale validations of sophisticated ansatz design, showing that incorporating physical intuition can dramatically improve quantum algorithm performance. This work could influence how researchers design variational circuits across quantum chemistry, materials science, and optimization applications where understanding the underlying physics can guide more efficient quantum computations.

The performance of the variational quantum eigensolver depends critically on the choice of ansatz. In this work, we experimentally evaluate the emergent-coupling-based ansatz (ECBA), a physically motivated variational ansatz for disordered systems. The ECBA is based on a renormalization (semi-)group approach to determine the dominant effective couplings, resulting in shallow circuits that capture the essential long-range entanglement structure while balancing local correlations. We implement the ECBA on superconducting quantum processors and benchmark it on disordered Heisenberg chain models. Using classically pre-optimized parameters and error mitigation techniques, we study systems of up to 30 qubits and observe an experimental relative energy accuracy of 96.47% for the largest system. Furthermore, we find that the ECBA can be efficiently embedded on hardware with two-dimensional square-lattice connectivity. We compare to commonly used hardware efficient ansätze and observe that the ECBA achieves significantly higher accuracy at a similar gate count.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UMass Amherst Research Demonstrates New Technology for Shrinking Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial step toward making quantum computers more practical by addressing one of their biggest challenges: their enormous physical footprint. Just as the miniaturization of classical processors in the 1970s enabled the personal computer revolution, shrinking quantum hardware could similarly transform the field from laboratory curiosities to deployable systems. The work on integrated laser and ion trap components is particularly significant because trapped-ion quantum computers are among the most promising architectures for near-term applications, but currently require room-sized setups with complex optical systems. If successful, this miniaturization approach could accelerate the timeline for quantum computers to move out of specialized facilities and into more accessible environments.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Scientists in the Riccio College of Engineering at the University of Massachusetts Amherst and the University of California Santa Barbara have demonstrated key laser and ion trap components necessary to help drastically shrink the size of quantum computers, an achievement aligned with the shrinking of integrated microprocessors in the 1970s, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Phase statistics of a single qubit emission as a direct probe of its coherence
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work demonstrates a clever new way to directly measure quantum coherence by analyzing the phase statistics of light emitted from a superconducting qubit, essentially turning the qubit's photon emission into a real-time coherence detector. What makes this particularly valuable is that researchers can now separate and independently measure both energy relaxation and pure dephasing processes by examining how the emission's amplitude decays versus how its phase distribution broadens over time. The technique works even in high-noise environments and provides a more direct window into decoherence mechanisms than traditional methods, which could prove invaluable for characterizing and improving qubit performance in practical quantum computing systems. This represents an important advance in quantum measurement techniques that bridges fundamental physics with practical qubit diagnostics.

The emission of photon from an individual atom encodes the phase of its initialized quantum state. Using single-shot heterodyne detection, we measure the phase distribution of the emission from a superconducting transmon qubit in an open waveguide configuration and track its evolution over time. We demonstrate that the presence of a quantum superposition is encoded in the phase statistics of the emission and remains resolvable despite a high noise level. These phase statistics serve as a quantitative probe of the qubit coherence. The decay of the emission envelope with increasing integration time reveals the energy relaxation rate of the emitted wavepacket, while phase distribution broadening tracks pure dephasing processes. We thereby establish a direct link between the decoherence dynamics of an open quantum system and the statistical properties of its radiated field.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: What Quantum AI Actually Means
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a crucial topic as quantum AI hype reaches fever pitch across the tech industry, offering much-needed clarity on what quantum computing can realistically contribute to artificial intelligence applications. With major tech companies and quantum startups making bold claims about quantum speedups for machine learning, understanding the actual state of quantum AI research becomes essential for separating genuine breakthroughs from marketing buzz. The piece appears to address fundamental questions about where quantum advantages might emerge in AI workflows and what current limitations exist, providing valuable perspective as the field moves from theoretical promise toward practical implementation. This type of analysis is particularly important now as quantum computers begin demonstrating real capabilities, helping readers understand which quantum AI applications might deliver near-term value versus those that remain years away.

Insider Brief The convergence of quantum computing and artificial intelligence has captured significant attention from researchers, investors, and media outlets. Headlines frequently suggest that quantum AI represents the next revolution in computing, positioning it as either the successor to current AI systems or a radical enhancement that will unlock capabilities classical computers cannot achieve. However, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Towards Analyzing Formic Acid Using Classical and Quantum Methods
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications by combining multiple computational approaches to study formic acid formation, a process crucial for both carbon capture technologies and understanding the origins of life. The researchers' integration of variational quantum eigensolvers with a novel "discrete quantum exhaustive search" method represents an intriguing attempt to circumvent the notorious barren plateau problem that has plagued quantum chemistry simulations on current devices. While the study focuses on a relatively simple molecular system, this hybrid classical-quantum approach could provide a roadmap for making quantum advantage in chemistry more practically achievable. The work stands out for directly connecting fundamental quantum algorithm development to urgent real-world challenges like carbon footprint reduction, demonstrating how quantum computing research is increasingly aligning with sustainability goals.

Catalytic carbon fixation to formic acid is important for studying the reduction of carbon footprint and the emergence of life. Can discrete quantum exhaustive search merged with other methods help reduce the carbon footprint? We suggest merging quantum, quantum inspired, and classical tools for a better simulation of various relevant processes. Quantum tools are often used for analyzing the electronic structure of molecules, sometimes because this problem is not scalable (in the number of orbitals) on classical computers while it is potentially approximately scalable on (future) quantum computers. It is potentially even solvable in the near future using variational quantum eigensolvers (VQE) yet a major obstacle to such analysis is the appearance of barren plateaus in the Hilbert space describing the problem. Here we make use of the basic (standard) tools while also including a novel one -- the discrete quantum exhaustive search, which relies on mutually unbiased bases, for analyzing the simplest non-catalytic process involving carbon dioxide, hydrogen and formic acid.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Compact Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution System Employing Monolithically Integrated Silicon Photonic Transceiver
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates a major step toward practical quantum cryptography by achieving the first continuous-variable quantum key distribution system using a fully integrated silicon photonic chip. The 1.9 Mbit/s secret key rate over 25 km represents impressive performance for a monolithic system, suggesting that quantum-secure communications could soon be deployable using standard semiconductor manufacturing processes rather than bulky laboratory setups. By integrating both the transmitter and receiver on a single silicon chip, this work addresses one of the biggest barriers to widespread QKD adoption: the need for expensive, specialized hardware that's difficult to manufacture at scale. This electronic-photonic integration approach could pave the way for quantum-secure communications to be embedded directly into everyday networking equipment.

We demonstrate the first CV-QKD system featuring a custom-designed monolithic silicon photonic dual-polarisation transceiver. Leveraging PS-64-QAM, we achieved 1.9 Mbit/s secret key rate across 25 km of standard single-mode fibre, highlighting the potential of electronic-photonic integration for practical QKD.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Local robust shadows on a trapped ion computer -- a case study
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article demonstrates a practical solution to one of quantum computing's most persistent challenges: measurement errors that corrupt the readout of quantum states. The researchers successfully implemented "local robust shadows" on a trapped-ion quantum computer, showing how alternating calibration and measurement phases can effectively correct for these errors even when measurement pulses are deliberately shortened to speed up experiments. What makes this particularly exciting is that they tested the protocol on realistic quantum algorithm outputs, including QAOA states used in optimization problems, proving the technique works beyond toy examples. This represents genuine progress toward making quantum computers more reliable and faster simultaneously - typically a challenging trade-off that this error mitigation approach helps resolve.

We experimentally demonstrate local robust shadows on a trapped-ion device, a protocol developed to counteract measurement errors. We alternate between a calibration stage and the shadow estimation stage and also introduce Pauli-X-twirling before measurements in both stages to symmetrize error rates. We then demonstrate the protocol on a trapped-ion quantum computer with artificially shortened measurement pulse duration. This yields faster experiments at the cost of increased error rates which are subsequently mitigated by the robust shadow protocol. We benchmark this approach on three exemplary quantum states: a local Haar random state, as well as standard and Pauli-correlation encoded QAOA states. In all three cases, the local robust shadow protocol succeeds at mitigating the increased error rates hailing from shorter measurement pulse durations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Toshiba and LQUOM Partner on Long-Distance Quantum Communication Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Toshiba and LQUOM addresses one of the most pressing challenges in quantum communication: maintaining quantum key distribution security over long distances where quantum signals naturally degrade. Toshiba brings deep expertise in QKD systems and has been a pioneer in quantum cryptography for over two decades, while LQUOM specializes in quantum repeater technologies essential for extending quantum networks. The collaboration is particularly significant as it could help bridge the gap between current metropolitan-scale quantum networks and the ultimate goal of intercontinental quantum communication infrastructure. Success in long-distance QKD extension would be a crucial step toward building the quantum internet that governments and enterprises need for truly unbreakable communications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Toshiba Corporation (Headquarters: Kawasaki, Kanagawa, Japan; President and CEO: Taro Shimada; hereinafter “Toshiba”) and LQUOM Inc. (Headquarters: Yokohama, Kanagawa, Japan; CEO: Kazuya Niizeki; hereinafter “LQUOM”) have entered into a joint research agreement to explore the extension of quantum key distribution (QKD) over long distances. This collaboration forms part of broader […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum engineering with ultracold polar molecules using trap-induced resonances
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research presents a clever paradigm shift in quantum computing with polar molecules by turning a typical obstacle into an advantage. While motional dephasing from optical traps is usually viewed as a problem that degrades quantum operations, these researchers demonstrate how the trap structure itself can be engineered to create controllable resonances for implementing quantum gates. This approach could significantly advance polar molecule platforms for quantum simulation, as it provides a new pathway for precise control without requiring the traditional methods of suppressing all motional effects. The work is particularly exciting because polar molecules offer unique advantages like long-range dipolar interactions and rich internal structure, making this trap-engineering technique a valuable addition to the quantum computing toolkit.

Polar molecules represent a promising platform for quantum simulation and computation protocols. Highly controllable arrays of optical tweezers are now accessible in experiments, allowing for unprecedented control of individual molecules. Motional dephasing is typically seen as an obstacle in quantum computing scenarios. Here, we instead consider using the trap structure as a resource for implementing efficient quantum gates. By numerically solving the two-body problem of dipoles trapped in separate tweezers, we identify trap-induced resonances that can serve as the mechanism for achieving state-dependent dynamics and can be further utilized for quantum sensing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-Coherent Regime of Programmable Dipolar Spin Ice
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a major breakthrough in quantum simulation, as researchers have successfully created the first quantum-coherent version of artificial spin ice using over 400 superconducting qubits. What makes this particularly exciting is their observation of "super-diffusive" transport of magnetic monopole-like excitations, revealing quantum dynamics that go beyond what's possible in classical systems or previous artificial spin ice experiments. The ability to program and directly observe these exotic quantum phases opens up new possibilities for studying emergent phenomena that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work demonstrates how quantum annealers can serve as powerful platforms for exploring complex many-body physics that would be impossible to study otherwise, potentially leading to insights about quantum materials and novel quantum computing approaches.

Frustrated spin-ice systems support emergent gauge fields and fractionalized quasiparticles that act as magnetic monopoles. Although artificial platforms have enabled their direct visualization, access to their quantum-coherent dynamics has remained limited. Here we realize a programmable dipolar square spin-ice model using a superconducting-qubit quantum annealer, providing access to a previously unexplored quantum-coherent regime of artificial spin ice. By implementing a direct one-to-one mapping between lattice spins and physical qubits, together with engineered extended couplings, we realize effective dipolar interactions on frustrated lattices comprising more than 400 vertices. Tuning transverse-field fluctuations enables us to probe the real-time dynamics of Dirac-string defects and interacting monopole plasmas. We observe super-diffusive monopole transport, with scaling exponents intermediate between classical diffusion and ballistic motion, indicating dynamics beyond classical stochastic relaxation and consistent with coherent propagation within an emergent gauge manifold. These results establish programmable quantum spin ice as a scalable platform for investigating fractionalized excitations and emergent gauge dynamics in engineered quantum matter.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Device independent quantum key distribution with robust self-tests
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum cryptography's biggest challenges by bridging the gap between device-independent quantum key distribution (DIQKD) and practical implementations. While DIQKD offers the strongest security guarantees by making minimal assumptions about the hardware, it has been notoriously difficult to implement in real-world conditions due to demanding requirements like loophole-free Bell tests. The researchers propose an elegant solution using "routed Bell-test setups" that allow parties to perform rigorous self-tests of their equipment locally, essentially bringing DIQKD's robust security framework down to earth for practical deployment. This approach could finally make ultra-secure quantum key distribution accessible beyond specialized laboratory settings, representing a significant step toward commercializing quantum cryptography's most theoretically sound protocols.

Device-independent quantum key distribution (DIQKD) provides a model of quantum key distribution with minimal assumptions and highly abstract theoretical building blocks. Although DIQKD frees us from detailed discussions of specific device models and associated error parameters, it replaces them with fundamental assumptions about the validity of quantum experiments. In this work, we propose a way to lift a protocol based on DIQKD-style assumptions to a device-dependent QKD protocol by performing local self-tests in the laboratories of the two key-generating parties. In particular, we consider routed Bell-test setups as a means of self-testing the local parties in earnest and develop a rigorous mathematical framework showing that the underlying optimization problems can indeed be transferred to the device-dependent QKD setting. As an application, we illustrate many of the relevant techniques through the case study of a routed BB84 protocol.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement generation of arbitrary squeezed Fock states
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research presents a sophisticated method for creating entanglement between superconducting qubits and squeezed cavity states, which represents a significant advance in controlling non-Gaussian quantum states that are notoriously difficult to generate and manipulate. The work is particularly notable because it achieves high-fidelity entanglement through an adiabatic process that slowly sweeps the cavity frequency, making the protocol robust against experimental imperfections that typically plague quantum state preparation. These complex entangled states between qubits and multi-photon cavity modes could prove essential for fault-tolerant quantum computing architectures and enable quantum sensors that surpass classical measurement limits. The combination of theoretical rigor using high-order time-averaging methods and demonstrated robustness through numerical simulations suggests this approach could be practically implemented in current superconducting quantum platforms.

We propose an efficient and robust protocol for the generation of entanglement between a superconducting qubit and a squeezed cavity. By applying a parametric drive to the cavity coupled to the qubit, the dynamical evolution of the system is precisely described by an anisotropic Rabi model within a squeezed reference frame. Utilizing high-order time-averaging methods, we analytically derive the resonance conditions and the effective Rabi frequency for the high-order three-photon process. By implementing an adiabatic passage, slowly tuning the cavity frequency across the resonance, the system is steered into a maximally entangled state, e.g., between the three-photon state $\ket{g,3}$ and the qubit excited state $\ket{e,0}$ in the squeezed picture. Numerical simulation results confirm the high fidelity and robustness of the proposed protocol. Our method provides a practical pathway for generating complex non-Gaussian entangled states, which are of significant value for fault-tolerant quantum computation and quantum metrology beyond the standard quantum limit.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Resonance fluorescence of an artificial atom with a time-delayed coherent feedback
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article reports a fascinating breakthrough in quantum optics that challenges one of the field's most fundamental assumptions - the Markovian approximation that treats quantum systems as "memoryless." By introducing deliberate time delays into a superconducting qubit feedback loop, researchers have demonstrated the first experimental observation of Mollow triplets in the non-Markovian regime, where the system's quantum memory creates entirely new spectroscopic signatures that can't be explained by conventional theory. This work opens exciting possibilities for quantum technologies that exploit memory effects, potentially leading to more robust quantum networks and novel quantum information processing protocols that harness non-Markovian dynamics. The ability to controllably engineer quantum memory effects in superconducting circuits could become a powerful new tool for both fundamental quantum science and practical quantum device engineering.

The model of light-matter interaction in quantum electrodynamics typically relies on the Markovian approximation, which assumes that the system's future evolution depends solely on its current state, effectively treating it as a ``memoryless" process. However, this approximation is not valid in scenarios when retardation effects are significant. These memory and retardation effects have the potential to improve existing quantum technologies (e.g., large-scale quantum networks, quantum information processing) and unlock new phenomena for future applications. In this work, we show theory and experiments of a time-delayed coherent feedback system using a transmon artificial atom (treated as a qubit) embedded in a superconducting circuit waveguide, in both linear and nonlinear excitation regimes. By using a feedback loop with a delay time comparable to the qubit relaxation time, pronounced non-Markovian effects appear in the dynamics of the qubit evolution. We also show how the resonance fluorescence spectrum, including elastic and inelastic scattering (such as the well-known Mollow triplet), can be significantly modified through the interaction between the qubit and feedback loop to show genuine non-Markovian and quantum nonlinear phenomena that cannot be explained with instantaneous coupling parameters. This work presents the first experimental report of Mollow triplets in the non-Markovian regime.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Opens Early Access to ‘Willow’ Quantum Processor, Invites Experimental Proposals
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a pivotal shift from Google's previous approach of keeping quantum hardware primarily in-house, suggesting the company is confident enough in Willow's capabilities to begin external collaboration. Opening early access to researchers represents a crucial step toward building the quantum software ecosystem needed for practical applications, as developers can now experiment with algorithms on Google's error-corrected quantum processor. The move positions Google to accelerate the development of quantum algorithms across multiple domains while potentially establishing Willow as the de facto platform for quantum research, similar to how cloud computing platforms became essential infrastructure for classical computing development. This strategy could significantly compress the timeline between quantum hardware breakthroughs and real-world applications by enabling parallel development of both the processor technology and the software stack needed to run on it.

Insider Brief This week, Google spread some tantalizing morsels signaling its push toward quantum commercialization is accelerating, from exploring neutral atom computing to shortening its timeline for post-quantum security. Now, a brand new announcement indicates its Willow processor is ready for another concrete step in that direction. Google just announced it is opening limited early […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Chinese Researchers Demonstrate Logical Operations on Silicon Quantum Processor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial step toward making quantum computers manufacturable at scale, as silicon-based processors can leverage the same fabrication infrastructure that produces today's classical chips. The demonstration of universal logical operations with error correction in silicon addresses one of the field's biggest challenges - moving beyond fragile physical qubits to robust logical qubits that can actually perform reliable computations. What makes this particularly significant is that it combines two essential elements for practical quantum computing: the stability needed for error correction and the manufacturing compatibility required for commercial viability. While still in early stages, this work from Chinese researchers suggests that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computers may run through familiar silicon territory rather than exotic materials.

Insider Brief A team of researchers has demonstrated universal logical operations in a silicon-based quantum processor, showing that error-corrected quantum computing can be implemented in a platform compatible with existing semiconductor manufacturing. The team, led by scientists from Shenzhen International Quantum Academy, write that this advance can be seen as a requirement for practical quantum […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Guest Post — Quantum Digital Twins: The Missing Acceleration Layer for Quantum Hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: the massive gap between theoretical quantum algorithms and the messy realities of current quantum hardware. Quantum digital twins represent a crucial middleware layer that could dramatically accelerate the field by creating high-fidelity simulations of actual quantum devices, complete with their unique noise profiles and error characteristics. Rather than spending months tweaking algorithms on expensive, queue-limited quantum computers, researchers could rapidly prototype and optimize on these digital replicas before deploying to real hardware. This approach could be the key to unlocking practical quantum advantage in the near term, transforming quantum computing from an experimental curiosity into a reliable engineering platform.

Guest Post By Izhar Medalsy, CEO and cofounder, Quantum Elements and Prof. Daniel Lidar, CSO and cofounder, Quantum Elements Most informed observers believe quantum is a transformative technology that will have a huge impact on people’s lives and likely play a pivotal role in solving many of the problems facing humanity today. However, hardware progress […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Xanadu Becomes First Pure-Play Photonic Quantum Computing Company to Go Public
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone marks a significant moment for the quantum computing industry, as Xanadu becomes the first pure-play photonic quantum computing company to achieve a public listing, validating photonic approaches as a legitimate competitor to superconducting and trapped-ion architectures. The public debut provides Xanadu with crucial capital to scale their room-temperature quantum systems and advance their cloud-accessible quantum computers, potentially accelerating development of practical photonic quantum applications. This move signals growing investor confidence in alternative quantum computing modalities beyond the more established superconducting systems, and could pave the way for other photonic quantum startups to seek public funding. The listing also brings much-needed visibility to photonic quantum computing, which offers inherent advantages like network connectivity and room-temperature operation that could prove crucial for distributed quantum computing applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Xanadu Quantum Technologies Limited (“Xanadu” or the “Company”), a leading photonic quantum computing company, today became a publicly listed company on Nasdaq and Toronto Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol “XNDU,” following the completion of its previously announced business combination with Crane Harbor Acquisition Corp., a special purpose acquisition company. […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant milestone in quantum computing's journey from laboratory curiosity to practical scientific tool, demonstrating that today's quantum computers can accurately reproduce experimental data from national laboratories for real magnetic materials. The ability to simulate quantum materials using quantum computers addresses one of the field's most promising near-term applications, where quantum systems naturally excel at modeling other quantum phenomena that overwhelm classical computers. This validation against established experimental benchmarks suggests we're approaching the point where quantum simulators could accelerate materials discovery, potentially leading to breakthroughs in everything from more efficient solar cells to room-temperature superconductors. The work bridges the gap between quantum computing theory and real-world materials science, offering a glimpse of quantum advantage in a domain where it could have transformative impact.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of the most fundamental challenges in quantum computing: creating and maintaining entanglement at scale, which is essential for quantum computers to achieve their theoretical advantages over classical systems. While the summary touches on quantum computing's transformative potential, the real significance lies in addressing the engineering hurdle of preserving delicate quantum states across hundreds or thousands of qubits simultaneously. Current quantum computers are limited by decoherence and noise that destroys entanglement, making scalable entanglement generation a critical milestone for moving beyond today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices toward fault-tolerant quantum computers. Breakthroughs in this area could be the key that unlocks practical quantum advantage for real-world applications like drug discovery and cryptography.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dual-rail superconducting qubits generate high-fidelity logical entanglement, study finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant step forward in quantum error correction, as dual-rail encoding offers a promising pathway to more robust qubits that can better preserve quantum information against noise and decoherence. The achievement of high-fidelity logical entanglement is particularly noteworthy because it demonstrates that these encoded qubits can maintain their quantum properties while being manipulated, which is essential for building fault-tolerant quantum computers. Superconducting platforms have been leading the charge in quantum computing scalability, and this work suggests that dual-rail architectures could provide the reliability needed to move beyond current noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices toward truly practical quantum computers.

Quantum computers, systems that process information leveraging quantum mechanical effects, could outperform classical computers on some advanced tasks. These systems rely on qubits, the fundamental units of quantum information, that become linked via an effect known as quantum entanglement and share a unified quantum state.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws. Unlike typical quantum systems where entanglement grows with the boundary area between regions, Motzkin chains exhibit logarithmic scaling that makes them notoriously difficult to simulate on classical computers, yet they hold deep connections to topological phases and even string theory through AdS/CFT correspondence. The researchers' proposed Rydberg atom platform offers a realistic experimental route to create these mathematical constructs in the lab, potentially opening doors to studying a whole new class of highly entangled quantum phases that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work exemplifies how programmable quantum simulators are becoming powerful tools for exploring exotic quantum matter that exists at the edge of what's computationally tractable.

Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: NRFC Investment Accelerates SQC’s Atomic-Scale Manufacturing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Silicon Quantum Computing's unique atomic-scale manufacturing approach, which positions them as the sole company worldwide capable of fabricating quantum chips with atomic precision. The AUD$20 million from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation not only validates SQC's silicon-based quantum technology but also strengthens Australia's position in the global quantum race, particularly in the critical hardware manufacturing sector where precision at the atomic level remains one of the field's greatest technical challenges. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's claim of already delivering quantum-enhanced AI products to enterprise and government clients today, suggesting their technology has moved beyond pure research into practical applications. This combination of manufacturing precision and commercial readiness could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computing deployment, especially in applications where silicon-based quantum systems offer advantages over other qubit technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) has invested AUD$20 million in Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC), a global leader in the field of quantum computing. SQC is the only company in the world capable of manufacturing quantum chips with atomic precision while delivering quantum enhanced-AI products for enterprises and governments today. It is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Global control via quantum actuators
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces an elegant solution to one of quantum computing's biggest architectural challenges: how to perform selective quantum operations without needing individual control over every qubit. The proposed quantum actuators act like smart intermediaries that can temporarily store and release energy on command, allowing processors to activate specific multi-qubit gates even when using simple global control fields that affect all qubits simultaneously. This approach could dramatically simplify the control electronics needed for large-scale quantum computers while actually enhancing their connectivity and enabling long-range quantum operations between distant qubits. The connection to quantum thermodynamics through the "quantum battery" interpretation adds theoretical depth and suggests these actuators might serve dual purposes in future quantum processors.

We introduce the concept of quantum actuators as mediators for globally controlled quantum computation. Auxiliary quantum systems act as controllable elements that transiently store and release interaction energy, enabling the selective activation of multi-qubit gates within globally driven architectures. During compilation they remain passive and require no fine-grained local control, while during operation they allow for controlled activation of interactions and directional flow of quantum information. We provide a framework for embedding quantum actuators in globally controlled processors, showing how they enhance connectivity, enable long-range entangling operations, and bridge distant regions without increasing local control overhead. We discuss physical implementations and architectural strategies illustrating how these elements extend the capabilities of global-control schemes. A complementary interpretation in terms of quantum batteries naturally emerges, connecting global-control architectures with concepts from quantum thermodynamics while highlighting the distinct operational role of quantum actuators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Lawmaker Proposes U.K.-U.S. Quantum Partnership Can be a Strategic Counterweight to China
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights how quantum computing is increasingly viewed through the lens of national security and international competition, with policymakers recognizing that quantum supremacy could reshape global power dynamics. Tugendhat's proposal for deeper U.K.-U.S. quantum collaboration reflects growing concerns about China's substantial investments in quantum research and the strategic importance of maintaining Western leadership in this critical technology. The mention of Quantinuum as a model partnership underscores how the quantum industry is already operating across borders, suggesting that formal government partnerships could amplify existing commercial and research ties. This signals a shift from viewing quantum computing as purely a scientific endeavor to recognizing it as a cornerstone of future economic and military competitiveness.

Insider Brief A transatlantic partnership between the U.S. and the U.K. could serve as a fulcrum in the global effort to develop quantum computing, with Quantinuum positioned as a leading example of how combined capabilities may accelerate progress and shape geopolitical competition. In a Wall Street Journal opinion column, Tom Tugendhat, a member of the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Study Finds More Qubits Yield Better Financial Optimization Outcomes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a crucial milestone in quantum computing's journey from laboratory curiosity to commercial viability, demonstrating that more qubits directly translate to better real-world performance in financial optimization problems. The collaboration between IonQ and Kipu Quantum shows quantum systems moving beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations to deliver measurable improvements over classical methods in portfolio optimization, one of the most promising near-term quantum applications. What makes this particularly significant is the clear scaling behavior observed when expanding from 36 to 64 qubits, suggesting that as quantum hardware continues to improve, financial institutions may soon see compelling reasons to adopt quantum solutions. This direct comparison with classical methods provides the kind of concrete evidence that enterprise decision-makers need to justify quantum computing investments.

Insider Brief Quantum hardware is beginning to show measurable gains on real financial problems, with larger qubit systems delivering better portfolio outcomes in direct comparisons with classical methods. A blog post, detailing results from a collaboration involving IonQ and Kipu Quantum, reports that increasing the size of executable quantum subproblems — from 36 to 64 […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: What Quantum Technology Actually is and Why it Matters
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article addresses a critical communication challenge facing the quantum industry: the tendency to oversimplify "quantum technology" as synonymous with quantum computing alone. While quantum computers capture headlines, quantum sensing and quantum communications are already delivering real-world applications in everything from medical imaging to secure financial transactions, yet they often get overlooked in mainstream discussions. As governments worldwide develop quantum strategies and allocate billions in funding, having precise terminology becomes essential for effective policy-making and public understanding. The piece serves as a valuable primer for distinguishing between quantum computing's future promises and the quantum technologies already transforming industries today.

Insider Brief The term “quantum technology” has entered mainstream discourse with increasing frequency, appearing in government policy documents, corporate strategy announcements, venture capital portfolios, and media headlines. However, the phrase often gets used imprecisely, sometimes referring exclusively to quantum computing while ignoring equally transformative developments in sensing and communications. Quantum technology is not a single […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Bell Experiments Revisited: A Numerical Approach Based on De Broglie--Bohm Theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This pedagogical study offers a fascinating numerical demonstration of how the de Broglie-Bohm interpretation of quantum mechanics can reproduce the famous Bell inequality violations that seem to rule out local hidden variable theories. By providing explicit simulations of particle trajectories and spin dynamics within this deterministic framework, the researchers bridge a crucial gap between abstract quantum theory and concrete visualization of entanglement phenomena. While de Broglie-Bohm theory remains a minority interpretation among physicists due to its requirement of nonlocal interactions, this work serves as an excellent educational tool for understanding how different interpretations can yield identical experimental predictions while maintaining fundamentally different pictures of physical reality. The numerical approach makes these subtle conceptual distinctions more accessible to students and researchers grappling with the foundational puzzles of quantum mechanics.

We present a complete and rigorous model of an EPR--Bell-type experiment within the framework of the de Broglie--Bohm theory. The purpose of this work is to show explicitly how a deterministic hidden-variable theory can reproduce all quantum-mechanical predictions, including the violation of Bell inequalities. Combining analytical arguments with numerical simulations, our approach offers a unified and transparent illustration of the central ingredients of de Broglie--Bohm theory, including particle trajectories, spin dynamics, and quantum entanglement. The model is designed to be pedagogical and self-contained, making it suitable for readers seeking a concrete understanding of how a nonlocal hidden-variable theory can describe the EPR--Bell experiment and illustrate Bell's theorem.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: 11 Photonic Quantum Computing Companies to Know [2026]
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive overview arrives at a pivotal moment for photonic quantum computing, as the field matures beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations toward practical systems that could sidestep many challenges facing superconducting and trapped-ion approaches. The diversity of strategies highlighted—from PsiQuantum's silicon photonic architecture to Xanadu's squeezed light approach—underscores how the photonic quantum landscape remains wide open, with no clear dominant paradigm yet emerging. What makes photonic systems particularly compelling is their potential for room-temperature operation and natural integration with existing fiber optic infrastructure, positioning them as strong candidates for distributed quantum networks and cloud-based quantum computing. The article's focus on 11 distinct companies also illustrates the significant commercial momentum building behind photonic quantum technologies, even as fundamental challenges like photon loss and deterministic single-photon generation continue to drive intense research and development efforts.

Insider Brief Photonic quantum computing uses photons instead of matter-based qubits, offering room-temperature operation, fiber-network compatibility, and multiple architectural approaches under active development. A global group of companies including PsiQuantum, Xanadu, and Quandela are advancing distinct photonic strategies spanning silicon photonics, squeezed light, and single-photon systems. Key technical barriers remain around photon loss, deterministic generation, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dynamical Evolution of Quantum Correlations and Decoherence in Coupled Oscillators Interacting with a Thermal Reservoir
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research provides crucial insights into how quantum information degrades in realistic noisy environments, which is fundamental to understanding the practical limits of quantum technologies.
